coredata.dc:title: Micro short circuit diagnosis for lithium-ion batteries based on Hellinger distance and inverse Markov method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Hong
Gao, Renjing
Chen, Zeyu
Tao, Qingyi
Zhang, Zilu
coredata.dc:description: Micro short circuits (MSCs) are minor faults within lithium-ion batteries and represent an early stage in the progression toward safety failures and thermal runaway. Early detection of MSCs is crucial for enhancing battery safety. However, this remains technically challenging, as the voltage anomalies caused by MSCs can easily be obscured by complex and frequent charging/discharging cycles. In this study, a two-step diagnostic approach is proposed for detecting MSC faults. Firstly, internal resistances are estimated using the forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method, and suspected MSC cells are identified based on the Hellinger distance calculated between each cell and a reference value. Secondly, the inverse Markov method is applied to analyze voltage transfer anomalies in the suspected cells, thereby confirming the faults. To support algorithm validation, MSCs are first induced through slight extrusion and the faulty cells are then operated in series with normal cells under the UDDS cycle, thereby establishing a labeled database containing both normal and MSC data segments. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using this dataset, and experimental results demonstrate that the method can effectively identify MSC faults, achieving a precision of over 98.0%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Micro short circuit
Safety management
Fault detection
Mechanical abuse

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Diagnosis based on the Hellinger distance
Results of the inverse Markov method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The flow diagram of proposed diagnostic method.
The implementation of Hellinger distance algorithm.
(A) m Markov state transition matrices. (B) The Markov matrix corresponding to voltage node k 1. (C) The diagram of voltage probability transfer.
Experimental platforms. (A) Extrusion system. (B) Battery testing system.
Test data of series-connected battery modules: (A) Module 1. (B) Module 2.
Resistance curves of six batteries under the UDDS cycle. (A) Internal resistances. (B) Zoomed-in view. (C) Reference resistance.
Diagnostic results under normal conditions. (A)–(D) represent four different diagnostic segments.
Diagnostic results of abnormal cases: (A) and (B) from Cell 5 and Cell 6 (MSC). (C) and (D) from Cell 9 and Cell 12 (MSC).
The diagnostic results based on Hellinger distance. (A) Module 1(including MSC battery 1). (B) Module 2(including MSC battery 2).
Diagnostic results: (A)–(C) Data segments from MSC battery. (D) Data segment from a normal battery. (E)–(H) Locally enlarged views for detailed illustration.
The diagnostic results based on both Hellinger distance and inverse Markov method. (A) Module 1(including MSC battery 1). (B) Module 2(including MSC battery 2).
The diagnostic results based on Entropy-based method. (A) Module 1. (B) Module 2.
Comparison of the proposed two-step approach with the only inverse Markov approach.

head.ce:title: Micro short circuit diagnosis for lithium-ion batteries based on Hellinger distance and inverse Markov method

Optimization and CAE Software for Industrial Equipment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117575, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117575, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Micro short circuits (MSCs) are minor faults within lithium-ion batteries and represent an early stage in the progression toward safety failures and thermal runaway. Early detection of MSCs is crucial for enhancing battery safety. However, this remains technically challenging, as the voltage anomalies caused by MSCs can easily be obscured by complex and frequent charging/discharging cycles. In this study, a two-step diagnostic approach is proposed for detecting MSC faults. Firstly, internal resistances are estimated using the forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method, and suspected MSC cells are identified based on the Hellinger distance calculated between each cell and a reference value. Secondly, the inverse Markov method is applied to analyze voltage transfer anomalies in the suspected cells, thereby confirming the faults. To support algorithm validation, MSCs are first induced through slight extrusion and the faulty cells are then operated in series with normal cells under the UDDS cycle, thereby establishing a labeled database containing both normal and MSC data segments. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated using this dataset, and experimental results demonstrate that the method can effectively identify MSC faults, achieving a precision of over 98.0%.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrification of transportation is regarded as a pivotal measure in energy reform aiming at addressing energy crisis and mitigating environmental pollution [1,2]. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in transportation electrification, such as electric vehicles (EVs) [3,4] and energy storage system, due to their high energy density, long cycle lifespan, and environmental friendliness [5,6]. However, numerous incidents involving EV fires, explosions, and spontaneous combustion have raised public concerns about the battery safety [7,8]. Adverse operating conditions, such as over-charge, over-discharge, and high temperatures, can deteriorate the battery performance and potentially lead to thermal runaway and other safety risks [9]. If such malfunctions are not identified in a timely manner, they may pose significant risks to both drivers and vehicles. Therefore, early detection and identification of potential faults are imperative for ensuring system safety [10].

Internal short circuits (ISCs) occurring inside LIBs are a common cause of thermal runaway [11,12]. Micro short circuits (MSCs) refer to an extremely small-scale ISC, typically caused by an internal defect generated during the manufacturing process or by external factors during operation, such as collisions or extrusions [13,14]. These may create microcracks in the battery separator, resulting in direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes [15,16]. Such damage or deformation is often undetectable or overlooked in practical applications, especially in minor collisions involving electric vehicles or unmanned battery application scenarios, such as unmanned vehicles, energy storage devices. Therefore, fault detection in early stage is necessary and of great significance for improving the battery safety. However, MSC detection remains technique challenging, as the voltage and current anomalies induced by MSCs are often submerged by complex and frequent charging/discharging load profiles, making it difficult to detect. [17,18]. To address this issue, various algorithms have been proposed for diagnosing LIBs, which can generally be categorized into three types: consistency-based method, model-based method, and data-driven method.

Consistency-based method is considered to be one of the most common and basic methods, and it is mainly to evaluate the differences among cells in the battery pack to determine if there is an internal short circuit in the battery pack. Ouyang et al. [19] proposed an internal short-circuit diagnosis algorithm, in which differential of the voltage and the fluctuation function of the internal resistance are calculated by RLS algorithm based on the mean-difference model (MDM). Zheng et al. [20] explored a novel method for ISC diagnosis based on SOC inconsistency. This method uses the mean SOC and the cell SOC differences estimated by extended Kalman filter (EKF) to achieve the ability to identify ISC faults. Moeini et al. [21] proposed a diagnostic algorithm that directly diagnoses ISC faults by comparing the voltage difference between each cell in a battery pack. When the voltage of one or several cells in the battery pack is obviously lower than that of the other cells for a period of time, it is diagnosed as an ISC fault. Lai et al. [22] proposed a SOC correlation-based early stage ISC detection method by calculating the correlation coefficient of SOC for adjacent cells. However, the accuracy of the above diagnostic methods can easily be affected. For example, ISC fault is not the only cause of voltage inconsistency, and the accuracy of estimated SOC will also decrease with factors such as battery aging. Therefore, diagnosis based on voltage inconsistency and SOC inconsistency will have a certain degree of misdiagnosis rate.

Model-based method is usually to transform the ISC fault diagnosis problem into a model parameter estimation problem, and compares the difference between the measured and the model-oriented parameters, thereby achieving ISC fault diagnosis. Feng et al. [23] estimated the SOC difference and the heat generation power from a three-dimensional electrochemical-thermo-ISC coupling model, and treated them as the fault indicators for ISC detection. Ma et al. [24] built the frequency-domain P2D model to calculate the diffusion coefficient and simulated the behavior of the impedance response with different levels of ISC. Feng et al. [25] adopted the equivalent circuit model and proposed an ISC detection algorithm to convert the measured voltage and temperature into an intrinsic electrochemical state that can reflect the short-circuit characteristics of the battery. Xu et al. [26] proposed a soft ISC fault detection method for LIB packs based on fault features extracted from incremental capacity curves. Xu et al. [27] employed a first-order RC equivalent circuit model to estimate the SOC and internal short circuit diagnosis. Due to noise, temperature, battery aging and other factors, building an accurate battery model is a challenging task. Moreover, the identification of model parameters requires a lot of computation, which is not suitable for online real-time fault diagnosis.

Data-driven method is based on a large number of battery state data generated during battery operation, and uses machine learning to diagnose the ISC fault of the battery [28]. Arunava et al. [29] proposed a novel ISC detection method based on an advanced machine learning approach. A set of ISC features of battery are identified and extracted from each charge-discharge cycle. Then a random forest classifier is trained and the accuracy of ISC fault detection is found to be more than 97%. Cui et al. [30] detected the ISC fault by combining electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a deep neural network (DNN), and the detection accuracy can achieve 97.5%. Xiao et al. [31] proposed a model for battery ISC detection, in which Gray relational analysis (GRA) is used to extract the features of the sample data, and random forest classifier (RFC) is used to realize the ISC diagnosis. Qiao et al. [32] developed a purely data-driven method using machine learning algorithms for diagnosing the early ISC fault based on 15, 30, 45, and 60-min relaxation voltage features. Recently, transfer learning has attracted growing attention in lithium-ion battery fault diagnosis. Since fault data are scarce and difficult to obtain in practice, especially for internal short circuits, conventional neural networks trained solely on limited labeled data often suffer from poor generalization. Transfer learning alleviates this problem by leveraging knowledge from source domains and adapting it to target domains with few samples. For instance, Sun et al. [33] proposed a transfer learning–optimized residual network with multi-label data processing, which enabled accurate diagnosis of ISCs even when battery parameters were unknown. Zhu et al. [34] integrated transfer learning into a multi–machine learning fusion framework, significantly improving the robustness and accuracy of internal short-circuit fault identification. These works not only reduce the dependence on large labeled datasets but can also enhance the algorithm adaptability across different types of batteries and varying operating conditions.

The above works provide valuable methods for fault diagnosis in lithium-ion batteries. However, the existing methods primarily rely on outlier analysis of abnormal data, and some even replace internal short circuits with external ones as substitutes. In this work, we establish a novel experimental approach in which ISCs are induced by mechanical compression, and the compressed batteries are mixed with normal batteries for charge-discharge tests. Based on this setup, a Markov-based diagnostic method is further developed and demonstrated. Unlike traditional statistics-based methods, the proposed method does not rely on amplitude thresholds, it leverages state transition anomalies for reliable fault detection instead. The main contributions of this study are summarized as follows. First, an anomaly detection method based on the Hellinger distance is established to sensitively identify abnormal patterns in battery responses. Second, a diagnostic strategy employing the inverse Markov method is proposed to improve the reliability of fault detection. Third, a labeled test database is constructed through compression experiments, which provides essential experimental support for validating the proposed algorithms. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the MSC fault diagnosis method and the specific algorithms. Section 3 presents the experimental data, diagnosis results and analysis. Finally, Section 4 summarizes the conclusions of this study.

A two-step fault detection method for MSCs in LIBs is proposed, and its overall workflow is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Due to the inherent delay in temperature measurement and the minor heat generation during the early stage of MSC, the resulting temperature variations are insufficient to serve as reliable diagnostic indicators. Instead, this work utilizes voltage and ohmic resistance as key features for anomaly identification. The experimental cells are 18650-type lithium-ion batteries with a nominal capacity of 2.5 Ah. The specific methodology is described as follows.

In the first step, Hellinger distance algorithm is applied to rapidly detect anomalies based on ohmic resistance. Firstly, the internal resistance of each cell is identified using forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method. In this study, a first-order RC model is adopted, as shown in Fig. 1. In this model, R p represents the polarization resistance, C p represents the polarized capacitor, OCV denotes open-circuit voltage, and U t denotes the terminal voltage. The internal resistance corresponds to the ohmic resistance of the LIB, denoted as R 0. Subsequently, a reference resistance value R re for the series-connected battery pack is determined as follows.

The battery cells are sorted according to their internal resistances. To reduce the influence of outliers, the maximum and minimum values are excluded. Then, the average of the remaining values is calculated and defined as the reference resistance, which is given by:
(1)
Q =
1
n − 2
(
∑
i = 1
n
R i
−
R max
−
R min
)
where n is the number of cells in the battery pack, R denotes the internal resistance while R max and R min are the maximum and minimum value, respectively.

Based on the above processing, the Hellinger distance between each target battery's internal-resistance distribution and the reference distribution is computed to quantify distributional similarity. For this calculation, the internal-resistance range φ is discretized into N bins with edges τ
j , where the edges are defined by
(2)
τ j
=
R min
+ j Δ R ,
Δ R =
R max
−
R min
N

Each bin is denoted as φ
j . The probability distributions of the internal resistance of each battery, relative to the reference resistance, are calculated by
(3)
{
P =
[
p 1
,
p 2
,
. . .
p j
,
. . .
p N
]
,
p j
=
∑
R ∈
φ j
R i
∑
R ∈ φ
R i
Q =
[
q 1
,
q 2
,
. . .
q j
,
. . .
q N
]
,
q j
=
∑
R ∈
φ j
Q i
∑
R ∈ φ
Q i

Internal resistance is adopted as the diagnostic basis, since a short-circuit induced by mechanical abuse leads to the deviation of the estimated resistance from that of the other normal cells. Although both voltage and resistance may serve this role, resistance is preferred because terminal voltage may be affected by polarization effects. Based on the probability distributions defined in Eq. (3), the Hellinger distance between each target battery and the reference is calculated as
(4)
H
(
P , Q
)
=
1
2
∑
j = 1
N
(
p j
−
q j
)
2

The Hellinger distance ranges from 0 to 1, where 0 indicates that the two distributions are identical, and 1 signifies complete dissimilarity. Based on this metric, an initial diagnostic threshold θ is established for anomaly detection. Specifically, Hellinger distances are calculated using a test dataset constructed by normal battery data, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Based on the statistical results, the maximum Hellinger distance observed among normal batteries is identified and adopted as the detection threshold to determine whether an anomaly has occurred.

In the second step, the inverse Markov method is conducted to achieve more reliable fault identification through voltage transition analysis. Unlike the conventional Markov approach, which predicts future states from current states and transition probabilities, the inverse Markov method evaluates observed transitions by checking whether their probabilities in the transition matrix are too small or nonexistent. Transitions falling into such improbable regions are regarded as fault events, thereby enabling fault detection. The Markov transition matrix is established in advance based on the voltage variation data from normal batteries. In this study, a m × m Markov voltage state transfer matrix is established based on the urban dynamometer driving schedule (UDDS) testing data of normal batteries. Specifically, the normal batteries undergo charging and discharging under the UDDS cycle. Considering that the low SOC range appears less frequently in actual vehicle applications, data from 3 to 3.2 V is discarded. Each 0.01 V voltage interval is recorded as a voltage node, thus, a total of m voltage intervals can be obtained in Eq. (5).
(5)
K =
[
k 1
k 2
. . .
k m
]
Here, m voltage nodes correspond to m Markov state transition matrices, as shown in Fig. 3 (A), and Fig. 3(B) shows the Markov state transition matrix corresponding to voltage node k 1. The range of voltage variation (4.2 V–3.2 V) is divided into m voltage segments, and the m elements in the Markov state matrix correspond to the probabilities that the current voltage node will jump to these m voltage segments at the next sampling time. Fig. 3(C) presents an example of voltage transfer between different Markov state transition matrices. m Markov state transition matrices can be calculated successively according to Eq. (6). Therefore, the voltage transfer characteristics between nodes through the m Markov state matrices are established.
(6)
A i
=
a 1
a 2
⋯
a j
⋯
a m
,
a j
=
∑
V
i , B j
∑
V
i , B
,
1 ≤ j ≤ N
where ∑V
i,B represents the number of voltages transferred to the voltage variation range (4.2–3.2 V) at the next sampling time under the voltage node k
i , and ∑V
i,Bj represents the number of voltages on the jth m-equal segment.

The Markov matrix is employed to characterize the probability distribution of voltage state transitions under normal operating conditions, where any transition falling into a zero-probability region is identified as a fault state. If the battery's voltage transfer probability (VTP) jumps to 0% probability (nonexistent) in the Markov matrix, it is considered an abnormal voltage segment, indicating a fault condition. It should be noted that this work focuses exclusively on cell-intrinsic defects. System-level failures [35], such as connector loosening or sensor malfunctions, could introduce additional disturbances to the algorithm. Distinguishing such faults poses another challenge, which will be the subject of our future research.

To verify the effectiveness of the proposed method, experiments were conducted to generate the dataset, which consists of two parts. The experimental setup is depicted in Fig. 4 . The extrusion equipment is used to produce the battery samples, including a WDW-300E extrusion machine, an upper computer controller, a HIOKI data acquisition instrument, and voltage/temperature sensors, as shown in Fig. 4(A). The extrusion force and deformation depth can be directly controlled via the host computer, and their data are recorded by the test system. In this process, the voltage and temperature are also collected by the data acquisition system. To better evaluate the response characteristics of the batteries with internal MSCs, the battery performance test platform is employed to obtain operating data from battery modules consisting of normal cells and MSC-faulted cells connected in series, as illustrated in Fig. 4(B). This setup comprises a hot chamber, a battery test system, a host computer, and a data recorder. It can simulate vehicle operating conditions, allowing the faulty battery to operate in series with the normal batteries, and records the voltage and current data for diagnostic application.

The experimental process is as follows: Firstly, extrusion abusing test is applied to produce batteries with internal MSC faults but not result in immediate failure. To obtain defective cells with internal MSCs, a slight compression was applied. This controllable process enables the reproducible induction of micro-short circuits for subsequent analysis. Extrusion experiments with depths ranging from 2 to 6 mm were conducted. When the extrusion depth reached 6 mm, the cell underwent an instantaneous safety failure accompanied by thermal runaway. In this study, we selected the cases of 4 and 5 mm, corresponding to the stage where extrusion induced internal short circuits but had not yet triggered thermal runaway. The internal short-circuit condition was confirmed by disassembling the cells and observing localized cracks and scorch marks on the separator. This process has been discussed in detail in Ref. [36] and is therefore not reiterated here. Subsequently, these defective batteries are connected in series to normal batteries and operate and subjected to charge/discharge cycle for gathering experimental data. Although the experimental validation is based on a limited number of extrusion-induced MSC samples, the collected dataset inherently spans a wide operating range. Specifically, MSC cells are connected in series with normal cells and exercised under dynamic driving profiles, yielding data across SOC levels from 100 % down to 20 %, which ensures that the diagnostic evaluation incorporates diverse SOC conditions and supports the robustness of the proposed method. In future work, the dataset will be further expanded by including more triggering conditions such as varied temperatures and deformation modes, so as to comprehensively validate the adaptability of the proposed method.

Two battery modules are constructed as summarized in Table 1 . Module 1 consists of five normal batteries and one MSC battery (deformation depth of 4 mm), while module 2 contains five normal batteries and one MSC battery (deformation depth of 5 mm). Both modules are operated under the UDDS cycle, during which the voltage and current data of each battery are collected in real time.

It should be noted that the diagnostic process is not implemented as an online procedure during the extrusion itself. Instead, the slight mechanical extrusion is applied to the battery in order to deliberately trigger an MSC under controlled conditions. Once the fault is initiated, the MSC battery is then connected in series with normal cells and subjected to the UDDS driving cycle. Fig. 5 presents the voltage curves of six cells in two series-connected battery modules during the UDDS cycle. The results show that cells with extrusion-induced MSCs can still operate stably under the UDDS cycle, and the voltage of the MSC cell (e.g., Battery 6) remains close to that of normal cells. This demonstrates that threshold monitoring alone is insufficient for accurate MSC diagnosis, highlighting the necessity of the proposed diagnostic algorithm.

By adopting this approach, we construct a well-labeled dataset in which the data segments corresponding to MSC cells and those corresponding to normal cells are clearly distinguished. Through repeated experiments under different extrusion depths and UDDS operational profiles, the dataset is further enriched and diversified, ensuring that it captures representative fault evolution characteristics. As a result, we obtain a comprehensive database that not only provides the foundation for training and validating diagnostic algorithms but also serves as a valuable benchmark resource for ongoing research on MSC detection and fault diagnosis in lithium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diagnosis based on the Hellinger distance
ce:section.ce:para: The internal resistance curves of the six target batteries can be identified by FFRLS method, as shown in Fig. 6(A) . For instance, one group of data segments from six different batteries are shown in Fig. 6(B), based on which the reference resistance can be obtained, shown in Fig. 6(C). On this basis, anomaly detection is performed using the Hellinger distance. Fig. 7 shows part of the detection results from the dataset labeled as normal batteries. For each data segment, 50 calculations of the Hellinger distance are performed. Each calculation is conducted over a 2,000-s time window, and the analysis is carried out in a rolling manner by shifting the window forward with a step size of 100 s. From each detection point circled in red in Fig. 7 and its enlarged views, it can be observed that the internal resistances of the normal batteries are relatively close to that of the reference value. Within the four diagnostic windows, there is no point that Hellinger distance values exceeding the normal threshold; therefore, they are correctly diagnosed as normal situations.

Fig. 8 shows the diagnostic results of abnormal cases. All four cases in Fig. 8 are diagnosed as abnormal. However, according to the ground-truth labels, Figs. 8(A) and (C) actually correspond to normal battery data, which were incorrectly identified as abnormal by the Hellinger distance. In contrast, Figs. 8(B) and (D) are labeled as faulty batteries, and the diagnostic results in these cases are correct. These results illustrate that the Hellinger distance alone may lead to false positives under certain conditions, as it is sensitive to transient fluctuations in voltage or resistance. The combination with the inverse Markov method in the proposed two-step framework helps reduce such misclassifications, thereby improving the overall reliability of fault diagnosis.

Fig. 9 shows the confusion matrix of the diagnostic results obtained by the proposed algorithm based on the Hellinger distance. The results indicate that all MSC batteries are accurately diagnosed, with no cases of MSC batteries remaining undetected. However, in Module 1 (which includes Battery 6 with an extrusion depth of 4 mm), 6.4% of the normal battery segments are misclassified as MSC segments, while in Module 2 (which includes Battery 12 with an extrusion depth of 5 mm), this probability is 5.6%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of the inverse Markov method
ce:section.ce:para: The voltage transition probability curves of suspected MSC batteries can be obtained based on the discharge voltage data and Markov state transfer matrix. If any point within the segment exhibits a voltage transition probability of 0%, the segment is diagnosed as a fault condition; otherwise, it is classified as a normal one. Fig. 10 shows the voltage transfer probabilities from battery data with the extrusion depth of 4 and 5 mm. It can be found in Fig. 10 that during the whole discharge process of the MSC batteries, there are several points jump to 0% probability of the Markov transfer matrix. For clearly seeing where the voltage probability jumps to 0%, we artificially mark the point with 0% Markov transfer probability as ‘-1’. Figs. 10(A)–(C) illustrate the diagnosis process of three data segments from the MSC batteries. The enlarged views in Figs. 10(E)–(G) clearly show the anomaly points. Figs. 10(D) and (H) show the results of data segments from a normal battery, where all monitoring points fall within the normal probability range. To improve the reliability of the diagnosis, this study adopts the occurrence of three consecutive abnormal points as the diagnostic criterion. It is worth noting that the required number of consecutive points directly affects the balance between sensitivity and robustness. A smaller number increases sensitivity but may also result in false positives due to transient fluctuations or noise. In contrast, a larger number improves robustness by filtering out random disturbances, but it can delay the confirmation of actual faults. Therefore, the choice of three consecutive points represents a balanced compromise between sensitivity and reliability. In this study, we also tested criteria with 2, 3, and 4 consecutive points, which yielded diagnostic accuracies of 97.7%, 98.0%, and 98.0%, respectively. The difference is minor for the current dataset; however, when the dataset expands to a larger scale, the selection of the number of consecutive abnormal points should be redesigned for optimal performance.

Fig. 11 shows the confusion matrix of the diagnostic results of the proposed algorithm based on Markov matrix. The results show that in Module 1, only 2.0% of the normal segments were misdiagnosed, and in Module 2, only 1.3% were misdiagnosed. The rate of misdiagnosis has significantly decreased, and the final fault detection accuracies of MSC battery with different extrusion depth of 4 and 5 mm are 98.0% and 98.7%, respectively.

The integration of the two methods is intended to enhance diagnostic reliability: the Hellinger distance improves anomaly coverage, thereby reducing missed detections, while the Markov method constrains state transitions to suppress false alarms. A comparison between the proposed two-step approach and one approach alone is shown in Table 2 . The results indicate that for the group with an extrusion depth of 4 mm, the proposed approach has a diagnostic accuracy rate that is 3.3% higher than that of the approach using the inverse Markov alone. For batteries with an extrusion depth of 5 mm, the accuracy rate is 3.0% higher.

The entropy-based battery fault diagnosis method is an advanced technique that applies information theory to quantify the complexity and uncertainty of the battery system [37]. Potential faults are identified by analyzing variations in the entropy values of operational data (e.g., voltage, current, temperature). The underlying principle is that a normally operating battery system maintains relatively stable entropy values, whereas the occurrence of faults induces sudden changes in data patterns, manifested as abnormal fluctuations in entropy. Specifically, a larger entropy value indicates greater data dispersion, while a smaller entropy value reflects reduced variability.

Fig. 12 presents the confusion matrix of diagnostic results using the entropy-based method. In Module 1, 4.7% of normal segments are misclassified, while Module 2 shows a false positive rate of 3.0%. The diagnostic accuracies of Module 1 and Module 2 are 95.3% and 97.0%, respectively, both lower than those achieved by the two-step diagnostic method proposed in this study. This demonstrates that the proposed two-step method provides higher diagnostic accuracy for MSC batteries compared with the entropy-based approach. While this study focuses on MSCs induced by mechanical abuse through extrusion tests, it is worth noting that MSCs can also originate from other factors, such as manufacturing defects or chemical aging. The present work adopts extrusion-induced MSCs mainly because they provide a controllable and repeatable way to generate labeled fault data for algorithm validation. Nevertheless, the proposed method is based on identifying abnormal resistance and voltage transition patterns, which are not unique to mechanically induced MSCs. This indicates that the method has the potential to be adapted for MSCs caused by other mechanisms, although further investigation and validation are required. Future work will therefore extend the analysis to MSCs induced by manufacturing and aging effects, in order to comprehensively evaluate the method's generality and limitations.

Mechanical stress-induced micro short circuits (MSCs) pose hidden safety risks in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and are challenging to detect. This paper proposes a novel two-step diagnostic algorithm for identifying such faults in LIBs. The method first applies the Hellinger distance algorithm to detect abnormal cells, followed by diagnosing abnormal voltage transitions using a Markov transition matrix. A mechanical stress abuse test is performed with an extrusion device to generate defective cell samples, and a battery pack containing both normal and extrusion-induced defective cells is then operated on a battery test system. The proposed method is evaluated using the resulting dataset. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed method achieves an MSC fault detection accuracy exceeding 98.0%, confirming its effectiveness for early fault identification. It should be noted that the present work emphasizes verifying the diagnostic effectiveness of the proposed method, whereas future studies will address its computational complexity and runtime evaluation on typical on-vehicle hardware to ensure real-time applicability in practical vehicle conditions. Overall, this approach has significant potential for enhancing battery safety management, particularly in identifying and mitigating hidden risks associated with mechanical stress-induced MSCs.

Hong Liang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Renjing Gao: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zeyu Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qingyi Tao: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Zilu Zhang: Writing – review & editing.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2341232 and U23B20139). Any opinions expressed in this paper are solely those of the authors and do not represent those of the sponsors.
